conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the eligibility rules so I can guide you properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I've reviewed the eligibility rules. To help determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who is 6 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help determine if you're eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, I have a few questions.

First, do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great. And do you live with Alex, or if not, do you contribute financially towards his upkeep (such as for food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. And I'm not currently contributing financially towards his upkeep at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Can I ask, does Alex live with someone else who may be looking after him (such as the other parent, a relative, or friend)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with his other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. This is an important factor in determining eligibility.

According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child, and you'll usually be considered responsible if either:
- You live with them, OR
- You're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money

Since Alex doesn't live with you and you're not currently contributing financially towards his upkeep, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for him at this time.

Just to confirm - is the other parent (who Alex lives with) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or planning to?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, his other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Based on the information you've provided, I can now give you a final determination on your eligibility.

Since Alex lives with his other parent (not with you), you're not contributing financially towards his upkeep, and his other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

The key rules are:
- Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child
- You're normally responsible for a child if you either live with them OR pay at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their upkeep
- His other parent is already receiving the benefit

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex comes to live with you, or if you start contributing financially towards his upkeep - your eligibility could change.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your circumstances?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), so meets the age requirement. However, the claimant does not live with Alex and is not contributing financially towards his upkeep. According to the eligibility rules, you're usually responsible for a child if either: (1) you live with them, or (2) you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. The claimant meets neither condition. Additionally, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has a 6-year-old child named Alex. However, Alex does not live with the claimant, and the claimant does not contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep. Alex lives with his other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the claimant does not meet the responsibility criteria (living with the child or contributing financially), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

The claimant meets neither condition. Additionally, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and has a 6-year-old child named Alex. However, Alex does not live with the claimant, and the claimant does not contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep. Alex lives with his other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the claimant does not meet the responsibility criteria (living with the child or contributing financially), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
